name: DPM failed to bring host out of standby mode
knowledgeSummary: This event records that Distributed Power Management tried to bring a host out of standby mode, but failed.Standby Mode powers off a host and allows it to be powered back on again through the Wake-on-LAN protocol. It can be triggered either manually or automatically by vCenter Server
knowledgeCause: There may be an issue with Wake-on-LAN (or the specific technology applicable to the hardware of this host). Check the host network and hardware status. Note - Do not disconnect a host in standby mode or move it out of the DRS cluster without first powering it on, otherwise vCenter Server is unable to power the host back on
knowledgeResolution: Power on the host using an alternative method and disable VMware DPM until the hardware issue is resolved

name: Host failed to exit standby mode
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the host failed to exit standby mode.Standby Mode powers off a host and allows it to be powered back on again through the Wake-on-LAN protocol. It can be triggered either manually or automatically by vCenter Server
knowledgeCause: Do not disconnect a host in standby mode or move it out of the DRS cluster without first powering it on, otherwise vCenter Server is unable to power the Host back on
knowledgeResolution: Power on the host using an alternative method

name: vCenter Server lost connection to host
knowledgeSummary: vCenter Server has lost connection to this host
knowledgeCause: This Alarm responds to events from vCenter Server indicating an issue with connecting to a specific host. The event IDs and details are: vCenter Server eventDescription HostCnxFailedAccountFailedEventFailed to configure management account HostCnxFailedAlreadyManagedEventAlready being managed by {VCServer} HostCnxFailedBadCcagentEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name} : Server agent is not responding HostCnxFailedBadUsernameEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Bad username/password HostCnxFailedBadVersionEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Incompatible version HostCnxFailedCcagentUpgradeEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Failed to install/upgrade vCenter Agent service HostCnxFailedEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Unexpected error connecting to host HostCnxFailedNetworkErrorEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Network error HostCnxFailedNoAccessEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Account has insufficient privileges HostCnxFailedNoConnectionEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Could not connect to host HostCnxFailedNoLicenseEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Not enough CPU licenses HostCnxFailedNotFoundEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Could not resolve hostname HostCnxFailedTimeoutEventConnection failed for {host.name} in {datacenter.name}: Timeout waiting for host response HostConnectionLostEventHost {host.name} in {datacenter.name} is not responding
knowledgeResolution: Please check the event description carefully as error resolution is dependent on the exact nature of the error
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host connection failure
knowledgeSummary: This alarm monitors the VMware vCenter Server API for events indicating that a host is disconnected
knowledgeCause: The host may be disconnected due to manual intervention, i.e. user-initiated disconnection in vCenter Server
knowledgeResolution: See the event description for more detailed information specific to this event. Check the status of the host in the vSphere Client. Check the network availability and configuration. Examine other open Alerts against this host in Veeam ONE Client that could indicate a failure. Ensure that the host is not due for scheduled downtime or maintenance
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM connection failure
knowledgeSummary: This VM is 'Disconnected' in vCenter Server
knowledgeCause: This usually indicates that the host itself is disconnected in vCenter Server
knowledgeResolution: Check status of vCenter Server and the host, e.g. network connectivity. Check the availability and configuration of back-end storage, e.g. SAN
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM CPU ready
knowledgeSummary: This VM has exceeded the threshold for CPU Ready Percent
knowledgeCause: CPU Ready Time is the time a VM waits in a ready-to-run state to be scheduled access to a (physical) CPU by host
knowledgeResolution: Some CPU Ready Time is expected, as host schedules its shared CPU resources. However, if high CPU Ready Time is seen for a VM, combined with slow response times and general poor end-user experience, it could be that the workload in the specific VM is more highly dependent on CPU response. Consider adjusting CPU shares and/or CPU limits for this VM, or moving it to another Host with less CPU-intensive activity
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM CPU usage
knowledgeSummary: This VM has exceeded the threshold for CPU usage
knowledgeCause: Usage is calculated as illustrated in the following example. Consider an 8-core host with a single 2-core VM. If each core is rated at 3,000 MHz and the VM consumes an average of 5,400 MHz over the interval, then the CPU Usage for that VM would be 90% (since 6,000 MHz is the maximum that a 2-core VM could consume and 5,400 is 90% of 6,000)
knowledgeResolution: Check the VM CPU usage tab.Check for high demand for CPU resources from application workloads inside the VM. Consider adding virtual CPUs to better distribute the processing across multiple cores. Note that multi-CPU VMs can impact the efficiency of the host CPU scheduler, as host must allocate all cores simultaneously in order to allow genuine multi-CPU activities within the VM
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: High balloon memory utilization
knowledgeSummary: There is high utilization of the VMware Tools memory controller, also known as the 'balloon driver', within this VM
knowledgeCause: VMware host employs two distinct techniques for dynamically expanding or contracting the amount of memory allocated to VMs - a VMware supplied balloon memory or vmmemctl module that is loaded into the VM OS as part of the VMware Tools and swapping pages from a VM to a server swap file without any involvement by the VM OS. The preferred mechanism is the balloon memory driver, which cooperates with the server to reclaim those pages that are considered least valuable by the VM operating system. This proprietary technique provides predictable performance that closely matches the behavior of a native system under similar memory constraints. It effectively increases or decreases memory pressure on the VM OS, causing the VM to invoke its own native memory management algorithms. When memory is tight, the VM OS decides which particular pages to reclaim, and if necessary, swaps them to its own virtual disk
knowledgeResolution: Check the host memory usage tab and VM memory usage tab.Note that high utilization of the balloon driver may not be a symptom of a performance or configuration problem per se. It could be considered normal operations for host to recover the least-used memory pages from the virtualized OS in this way. However if there is sustained high ballooning then it can indicate that the host is under high pressure for memory resources. You should examine related metrics such as memorySwap, available at both VM and host level. Note, high memory ballooning can cause the guestOS to use its page file, causing high disk I/O - see Host Disk I/O dashboard

name: VM memory swap usage
knowledgeSummary: This VM has exceeded the threshold for memory swapping to disk within the host
knowledgeCause: Swapping is used to forcibly reclaim memory from a VM when the VMware balloon memory driver is not available. This can occur if the VMware tools have not been installed in a VM or if the vmmemctl (balloon) memory driver has been disabled, and is not running or is temporarily unable to reclaim memory quickly enough to satisfy the VMs current demands. The balloon memory driver is used whenever possible. Swapping is a mechanism of last resort that the system uses to reclaim memory only when necessary. Note that this metric can remain stable for long periods, as ESX does not actively recover memory swapped to disk for performance reasons. A better indicator of memory resource issues in the swap file is memorySwapRate which shows continuous swapfile usage and is available for both ESX Hosts and VMs
knowledgeResolution: Check memory allocation, reservation, and limits for this VM. Check for memory overcommitment in host. Check for high memory demand within the VM Guest OS. Refer to VMware document Understanding Memory Resource Management in VMware ESX Server
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM disk SCSI connection failures
knowledgeSummary: This VMGuest disk connection (LUN) has logged one or more SCSI aborts
knowledgeCause: This alarm is configured to alert on any SCSI aborts. The guest systems use the SCSI protocol to communicate to disks, even over Fibre Channel to SAN LUNs. SCSI Bus aborts are in effect the SCSI subsystem timing out and commands being canceled. This happens when the HBA device is overloaded, or its q-depth is exhausted. The first thing to understand is which vmhba controller (C), target/path (T), and LUN (L) has experienced problems. The more VMs sharing a single LUN the more likely that aborts will occur. The rule of thumb is that no more than 10 VMs sharing a LUN, although performance of storage subsystems and VM workload demands mean this number may vary
knowledgeResolution: Check the status of the disk subsystem and connectivity. Check for possible disk hardware, firmware or driver issues. Check for high disk demand from applications inside this VM which may be stressing the available disk bandwidth. See historical graph of SCSI aborts
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM disk SCSI connection resets
knowledgeSummary: This VMGuest disk connection (LUN) has logged one or more SCSI bus resets
knowledgeCause: This alarm is configured to alert on any SCSI resets. The guest systems use the SCSI protocol to communicate to disks, even over Fibre Channel to SAN LUNs. SCSI Bus resets are issued to release resources. These SCSI Bus Resets are in effect the SCSI subsystem timing out, commands being canceled, and retrying. This happens when the HBA device is overloaded, or its q-depth is exhausted. The first thing to understand is which vmhba controller (C), target/path (T), and LUN (L) has experienced problems. The more VMs sharing a single LUN the more likely that resets will occur. The rule of thumb is no more than 10 VMs sharing a LUN, although performance of storage subsystems and VM workload demands mean this number may vary
knowledgeResolution: Check the status of the disk subsystem and connectivity. Check for possible disk hardware, firmware or driver issues. See historical graph of SCSI resets
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host CPU usage
knowledgeSummary: This host has exceeded the threshold for CPU usage.
knowledgeCause: This metric shows the averaged Total usage across all CPU instances (cores). One or more CPU instances has exceeded the threshold and brought the average CPU usage over threshold as well. CPU usage is calculated as illustrated in the following example. Consider an 8-core host with a single 2-core VM. If each core is rated at 3,000 MHz and a VM consumes an average of 5,400 MHz over the interval, then the cpuUsedPct for that VM would be 90% (since 6,000 MHz is the maximum that a 2-core VM could consume, and 5,400 is 90% of 6,000)
knowledgeResolution: View the host CPU usage tab for historical information. Check the allocation of CPU resources for VMs in vCenter Server. Check historical CPU usage, number of VMs running, and other performance factors. Check for alerts for VMs for high CPU usage

name: Host available memory
knowledgeSummary: This host is low on available memory
knowledgeCause: Host available memory is the percentage of total memory not being used. This is the memory host will use for new tasks, e.g. to start new VMs or to satisfy new memory demands from running VMs. This value has dropped below the configured threshold
knowledgeResolution: View the host memory usage tab for historical information. Check the allocation of memory resources for VMs in vCenter Server. Check historical memory usage, number of VMs running, and other performance factors. Check for alerts for VMs for high memory usage

name: Host memory pressure
knowledgeSummary: This host has exceeded the threshold for memory pressure
knowledgeCause: This metric is expressed as a percentage, which can go above 100%. It shows potential memory over-commitment issues on the host. This value is calculated internally in the Veeam ONE Monitoring Server, so is not visible in vCenter Server. Factors used in the calculation include Total allocated memory for powered-on VMs Memory overhead The effects of TPS (Transparent Page Sharing) The total available physical memory in the Host
knowledgeResolution: When this value exceeds 100% memory is generally referred to as "over-commitment". It may not indicate a performance problem as such, due to the efficient memory management features in hosts such as TPS. However, you should be aware of the potential performance impact. Should VMs demand memory then host must work to make memory available using methods such as the balloon memory driver, page-sharing, swapping to disk, etc. If this metric exceeds the threshold, then you should examine related metrics such as Memory SwapRate Memory Swapped Memory Shared Memory Available View the Host Memory Usage Dashboard for historical information

name: Host swap memory
knowledgeSummary: This host is swapping too much memory
knowledgeCause: Swapped memory is the sum of the currently swapped memory for all running VMs. Swapping is used to forcibly reclaim memory from a VM when the VMware balloon memory driver is not available. This can occur if the VMware tools have not been installed in a VM or if the vmmemctl (balloon) memory driver has been disabled, and is not running or is temporarily unable to reclaim memory quickly enough to satisfy the VMs current demands. The balloon memory driver is used whenever possible. Swapping is a mechanism of last resort that the system uses to reclaim memory only when necessary
knowledgeResolution: View the host memory usage tab for historical information. No resolution may be required. Note that this metric can remain stable for long periods, as host does not actively recover memory swapped to disk for performance reasons. A better indicator of memory resource issues in the swap file is memorySwapRate which shows continuous swapfile usage and is available for both hosts and VMs. Check also associated metric memoryPressure which shows host Memory Overcommitment

name: Host disk SCSI aborts
knowledgeSummary: This host disk (vmhba) has logged one or more SCSI aborts
knowledgeCause: This monitor is configured to alert on any SCSI Aborts. The host systems use the SCSI protocol to communicate to disks, even over Fiber Channel to SAN LUNs. SCSI Bus Aborts are in effect the SCSI subsystem timing out and commands being canceled. This happens when the HBA device is overloaded, or its q-depth is exhausted. The first thing to know is which vmhba controller (C), target/path (T), and LUN (L) experienced these problems. The more VMs sharing a single LUN the more likely that resets will occur. Rule of thumb is no more than 10 VMs sharing a LUN, although performance of storage subsystems and VM workload demands mean this number may vary
knowledgeResolution: Check the status of the disk subsystem and connectivity. Check for possible disk hardware, firmware or driver issues. See historical graph of SCSI aborts
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host disk bus resets
knowledgeSummary: This host disk (vmhba) has logged one or more SCSI bus resets
knowledgeCause: This monitor is configured to alert on any SCSI resets. The host systems use the SCSI protocol to communicate to disks, even over Fiber Channel to SAN LUNs. SCSI Bus Resets are issued to release resources. These SCSI Bus Resets are in effect the SCSI subsystem timing out, commands being canceled, and retrying. This happens when the HBA device is overloaded, or its q-depth is exhausted. The first thing to know is which vmhba controller (C), target/path (T), and LUN (L) experienced these problems. The more VMs sharing a single LUN the more likely that resets will occur. Rule of thumb is no more than 10 VMs sharing a LUN, although performance of storage subsystems and VM workload demands mean this number may vary
knowledgeResolution: Check the status of the disk subsystem and connectivity. Check for possible disk hardware, firmware or driver issues. See historical graph of SCSI resets
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Bad vCenter Server username logon attempt
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failed logon by user or third party application. The combination of username, password, and permissions is the mechanism by which vCenter Server authenticate a user for access and authorize the user to perform activities
knowledgeCause: Failed logons are due to no match existing between the provided username and password combination and the combinations stored for authentication
knowledgeResolution: As this alarm only alerts on repeated login failures, check for some automated process that is logging in with incorrect or expired password. In worst-case scenario, check for denial-of-service attack or attempted brute-force password guessing
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Bad Host username logon attempt
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failed logon by user or third party application. The combination of username, password, and permissions is the mechanism by which hosts authenticate a user for access and authorize the user to perform activities
knowledgeCause: Failed logons are due to no match existing between the provided username and password combination and the combinations stored for authentication
knowledgeResolution: As this alarm only alerts on repeated login failures, check for some automated process that is logging in with incorrect or expired password. In worst-case scenario, check for denial-of-service attack or attempted brute-force password guessing
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host operation cancelled
knowledgeSummary: An operation performed on the host was canceled
knowledgeCause: Typically, a previous event in the sequence of events contains more information about the cause of this cancellation
knowledgeResolution: Depending on the task that was cancelled, resolution may not be required. Check all previous events on vCenter Server
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host cluster destroyed
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a cluster is destroyed
knowledgeCause: This event records that a cluster was destroyed. This could be done by any user who has sufficient permissions to destroy clusters
knowledgeResolution: If an approved admin action within vCenter Server, then no resolution is required. If this was not approved, review security procedures and VMware events for the user who destroyed the cluster
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host cluster capacity overcommitted
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a cluster's host capacity cannot satisfy resource configuration constraints
knowledgeCause: Check that you have sufficient capacity in cluster to satisfy resource configuration in datacenter. A cluster becomes overcommitted when the tree of resource pools and VMs is internally consistent but the cluster does not have the capacity to support all resources reserved by the child resource pools
knowledgeResolution: Review the configured failover capacity value. The current failover capacity is calculated based on the number of VMs running and their reservations, and the resources being provided by the hosts in the cluster. When planning the size of HA clusters to provide the desired levels of failover capacity, keep in mind that each host requires some overhead memory and CPU and that each VM must be guaranteed its CPU and memory reservation
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Guest customization failure
knowledgeSummary: The customization sequence in the guest failed. Cannot complete customization of VM
knowledgeCause: See customization log on the guest OS for details
knowledgeResolution: Refer to the log file specified in the event message for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Linux customization identity failure
knowledgeSummary: Failed to set Linux identity
knowledgeCause: An error occurred while setting up Linux identity. See log file on guest OS for details
knowledgeResolution: Refer to the log file specified in the event message for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Network customization setup failure
knowledgeSummary: Failed to set Linux identity
knowledgeCause: An error occurred while setting up Linux identity. See log file on guest OS for details
knowledgeResolution: Refer to the log file specified in the event message for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Sysprep customization failure
knowledgeSummary: Sysprep failed to run in the guest during customization. This might have been caused by the fact that the wrong sysprep was used for the guest or errors in the sysprep file
knowledgeCause: The version of Sysprep provided for customizing VM does not match the version of guest OS. See the log file in the guest OS for more information
knowledgeResolution: Refer to the log file specified in the event message Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Customization unknown failure
knowledgeSummary: The customization sequence failed unexpectedly in the guest
knowledgeCause: An error occurred while customizing VM. For details reference the log file in the guest OS
knowledgeResolution: Refer to the log file specified in the event message for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Admission control disabled
knowledgeSummary: This event records when admission control checks have been disabled in a HA cluster
knowledgeCause: HA admission control disabled on cluster.Admission control runs when you power on a virtual machine, the system checks the amount of CPU and memory resources that have not yet been reserved. Based on the available unreserved resources, the system determines whether it can guarantee the reservation for which the virtual machine is configured (if any).Disabled admission control. If you select this option, virtual machines can, for example, be powered on even if that causes insufficient failover capacity. When this is done, no warnings are presented, and the cluster does not turn red. If a cluster has insufficient failover capacity, VMware HA can still perform failovers and it uses the VM Restart Priority setting to determine which virtual machines to power on first
knowledgeResolution: If this is a normal administrative operation, no resolution is required. The New Cluster wizard allows you to enable or disable admission control for the VMware HA cluster and choose a policy for how it is enforced

name: Admission control enabled
knowledgeSummary: This event records when admission control checks have been enabled in a HA cluster
knowledgeCause: HA admission control enabled on cluster.Admission control runs when you power on a virtual machine, the system checks the amount of CPU and memory resources that have not yet been reserved. Based on the available unreserved resources, the system determines whether it can guarantee the reservation for which the virtual machine is configured (if any).Enabled admission control enforces availability constraints and preserves failover capacity. Any operation on a virtual machine that decreases the unreserved resources in the cluster and violates availability constraints is not permitted
knowledgeResolution: If this is a normal administrative operation, no resolution is required. The New Cluster wizard allows you to enable or disable admission control for the VMware HA cluster and choose a policy for how it is enforced

name: Primary host connection re-established
knowledgeSummary: This event records that vCenter Server has re-established contact with a primary host in this HA cluster
knowledgeCause: Re-established contact with a primary host in this HA cluster
knowledgeResolution: No Resolution is required, this event only confirms that vCenter Server can communicate with an HA agent

name: Primary host unavailable
knowledgeSummary: This event records that vCenter Server cannot contact to any primary host in this HA cluster. vCenter Server has lost contact with all primary nodes with a connected state. Attempts to configure HA on a host in this cluster will fail until a DasAgentFoundEvent is logged or unless this is the first node to be configured. For example, if all the other hosts are disconnected first
knowledgeCause: Unable to contact a primary HA agent in cluster HA designates some hosts as primary hosts in the HA cluster. vCenter Server needs to contact the primary host for Cluster configuration actions
knowledgeResolution: In some cases, hosts report this error in vCenter Server. To ensure connection to the primary VMware HA node in the cluster: Re-configure VMware HA on the host that did not have the agent running. Wait for the reconfigure to complete. You receive an error similar to that generated on other hosts. From within vCenter Server disable VMware HA on the cluster. Wait for the reconfigure to complete across all hosts. Enable VMware HA for the cluster again.

name: All hosts in cluster isolated
knowledgeSummary: This event records that all hosts have been isolated from the network in a HA cluster
knowledgeCause: All hosts in the HA cluster were isolated from the network
knowledgeResolution: Check the network configuration for proper network redundancy in the management network. Note: By default, the isolated host powers off its virtual machines. These virtual machines can then successfully fail over to other hosts in the cluster

name: HA disabled for cluster
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a cluster has been disabled for HA
knowledgeCause: HA agent is either disabled by a user or lost connection to vCenter Server
knowledgeResolution: This may be an expected administrative action.If not and this is VC-initiated, it may indicate an error.Check network configuration on the clusterCheck connectivity to the vCenter Server from the ESX hostsTry to reconfigure HA and see if issue is resolved

name: HA enabled for cluster
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a cluster has been enabled for HA
knowledgeCause: HA enabled for cluster
knowledgeResolution: This may be an expected administrative action. Review vCenter Server events for more information on HA cluster creation

name: Host failure detected
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a host failure has been detected by HA
knowledgeCause: If a 15-second period elapses without the receipt of heartbeats from a host, and the host cannot be pinged, it is declared as failed. In the event of a host failure, the virtual machines running on that host are failed over, that is, restarted on the alternate hosts with the most available unreserved capacity (CPU and memory)
knowledgeResolution: Check host network connectivity and try to reconfigure HA

name: Host isolation in HA cluster
knowledgeSummary: This event records that a host has been isolated from the network in a HA cluster. Since an isolated host cannot be distinguished from a failed host except by the isolated host itself, this event is logged when the isolated host regains network connectivity
knowledgeCause: Host network isolation occurs when a host is still running, but it can no longer communicate with other hosts in the cluster. With default settings, if a host stops receiving heartbeats from all other hosts in the cluster for more than 12 seconds, it attempts to ping its isolation addresses. If this also fails, the host declares itself as isolated from the network
knowledgeResolution: Check host network connectivity and try to reconfigure HA.Note - If you ensure that your network infrastructure is sufficiently redundant and that at least one network path is available at all times, host network isolation should be a rare occurrence

name: DRS host standby mode entrance
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the host has successfully entered standby mode initiated by Distributed Power Management.A host in this mode has no running virtual machines and no provisioning operations are occurring
knowledgeCause: The reason VMware DPM evacuates hosts and powers them down to the Standby state is that hosts typically use 60 percent or more of their peak power when totally idle, so the power savings possible with this approach are substantial. Vacating a host and placing it in a lighter sleep state than S5, such as the ACPI S3 'suspend-to-RAM' state, can consume an order of magnitude more power than ACPI S5 because of the need to keep the host's RAM powered on
knowledgeResolution: If DPM is desired functionality within this cluster, then no resolution is required. Hosts powered off by VMware DPM are marked by vCenter Server as being in standby mode, indicating that they are available to be powered on whenever needed. vCenter Server will automatically power on the host (using Wake On LAN) when additional resources are required

name: DRS host standby mode exit
knowledgeSummary: This event records that Distributed Power Management brings this host out from standby mode
knowledgeCause: VMware DPM monitors the cumulative demand of all VMs in the cluster for memory and CPU resources and compares this to the total available resource capacity of all hosts in the cluster. If sufficient excess capacity is found, VMware DPM places one or more hosts in standby mode and powers them off after migrating their VMs to other hosts. Conversely, when capacity is deemed to be inadequate, DRS brings hosts out of standby mode (powers them on) and migrates VMs, using vMotion, to them. When making these calculations, VMware DPM considers not only current demand, but it also honors any user-specified VM resource reservations. Note - Before enabling VMware DPM on a host, it is important to manually test the 'exit standby' procedure for that host to ensure that it can be powered on successfully via one of the three protocols: wake-on-LAN (WoL), Intelligent Platform Management Interface (IPMI) or HP Integrated Lights-Out (iLO) technology
knowledgeResolution: If DPM is desired functionality within this cluster, then no resolution is required. Hosts powered off by VMware DPM are marked by vCenter Server as being in standby mode, indicating that they are available to be powered on whenever needed. vCenter Server has automatically powered on the host (e.g. using Wake On LAN) as additional resources are required within the Cluster

name: DRS invocation failure
knowledgeSummary: This event records DRS invocation failure. DRS invocation not completed
knowledgeCause: vCenter Server activates the DRS algorithm after a fixed interval (the default is five minutes) so DRS can make recommendations based on the past performance metrics of the VMs in the DRS cluster. For some reason the invocation has failed in this case
knowledgeResolution: Check DRS event history and log files. There may be an issue such as unavailable host, host in maintenance mode, or host which is in standby mode and will not respond to wake-on-LAN
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: DRS synchronization failure
knowledgeSummary: This event records when resource configuration specification synchronization fails on a host
knowledgeCause: Unable to apply DRS resource settings on host. Some aspect of configuring a host in the DRS cluster has failed. This can significantly reduce the effectiveness of DRS
knowledgeResolution: Check other alerts and alarms for this host and associated cluster. There may be a network, storage or other configuration issue. Check event history for DRS-related events
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Duplicate IP address detected
knowledgeSummary: This event records that a duplicate IP address has been observed, with conflict between VM, and the vMotion or IP storage interface configured on the host
knowledgeCause: VM has a duplicate IP
knowledgeResolution: No two network entities of any type should ever share the same IP address if they are on the same network. Check network records, and ensure that DHCP/DNS has not handed out the same IP to two network objects
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host HA disabled
knowledgeSummary: This event records when HA has been disabled on a host
knowledgeCause: HA agent is either disabled by a user or lost connection to vCenter Server
knowledgeResolution: If this is a desired admin action then no resolution is required

name: Host HA enabled
knowledgeSummary: This event records when the HA (high-availability) agent has been enabled on a host
knowledgeCause: HA agent enabled on host
knowledgeResolution: If this is a desired admin action then no resolution is required

name: Maximum host connections reached
knowledgeSummary: This event records if the inventory of hosts has reached capacity
knowledgeCause: Maximum number of hosts allowed for this edition of vCenter Server has been reached
knowledgeResolution: Make sure you do not exceed the number of hosts allowed for your edition of vCenter Server.Note you may need to update vCenter Server 4 - Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host IP inconsistent
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the IP address resolution returned different addresses on the host
knowledgeCause: Configuration of host IP address is inconsistent on host
knowledgeResolution: Make sure your DNS settings are correct
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host IP to short name failed
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the host's IP address could not be resolved to a short name
knowledgeCause: Cannot resolve IP address to short name on host
knowledgeResolution: Check your host's network configuration, DNS configuration, etc
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host Isolation IP not available
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the isolation address could not be pinged. The default isolation address is the service console's default gateway
knowledgeCause: Host could not reach isolation address. Host network isolation occurs when a host is still running, but it can no longer communicate with other hosts in the cluster. With default settings, if a host stops receiving heartbeats from all other hosts in the cluster for more than 12 seconds, it attempts to ping its isolation addresses. If this also fails, the host declares itself as isolated from the network
knowledgeResolution: Check if you are able to ping IP address stated at "das.isolationaddress" within the HA advanced options If "das.isolationaddress" is empty, ping default gateway for your host. If you're unable to ping neither of IP addresses, check if the Service Console default gateway on that host show up correctly in the vSphere client. Check in the Networking section of the Configuration tab for the host. Also ensure that your network infrastructure is sufficiently redundant and that at least one network path is available at all times, host network isolation should be a rare occurrence
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host license expired
knowledgeSummary: This event records an expired host license
knowledgeCause: A host license has expired
knowledgeResolution: Update your host license
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: No host network for HA available
knowledgeSummary: This event records the fact that a host does not have any available networks for HA communication
knowledgeCause: Host currently has no available networks for HA communication.To identify which network operations might disrupt the functioning of VMware HA, you should be aware of networks used for heartbeat and other VMware HA communications. On hosts in the cluster, VMware HA communications travel over all networks that are designated as service console networks. VMkernel networks are not used by these hosts for VMware HA communications. On hosts in the cluster, VMware HA communications, by default, travel over VMkernel networks, except those marked for use with vMotion. If there is only one VMkernel network, VMware HA shares it with vMotion, if necessary
knowledgeResolution: Specify networks in the HA Cluster settings that will be used for HA communication

name: Host not compliant
knowledgeSummary: This event records that host went out of compliance
knowledgeCause: Host is not in compliance with the attached profile
knowledgeResolution: To check a host profile compliance: Open vSphere Client In the Entities tab of the host profile main view, choose the host from the list under Associated Entity Click Check Compliance The compliance status is updated as Compliant, Unknown, or Non-compliant. If the status is Unknown or Non-compliant, apply a Host Profile to a host or cluster. Note: The host must be in maintenance mode before applying it to a profile. To apply a host or cluster to a profile: Open vSphere Client In the Host Profiles main view, choose the profile to which you want to associate the host or cluster. Click the host and clusters tab. The list of attached hosts and clusters are shown under entity name. Right-click on the entity and click Apply or click Apply Profile. The Compliance Status is updated
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: No host redundant management network available
knowledgeSummary: This event records the fact that a host does not have a redundant management network. It is recommended that host management networks be configured with redundancy
knowledgeCause: Host currently has no management network redundancy
knowledgeResolution: This message occurs if network redundancy is not configured properly (that is, if the Service Console does not have network redundancy). This message can be safely ignored if redundancy is not required. However, to prevent this message and to comply with proper network redundancy, VMware recommends that you add a second service console on a different vSwitch and subnet. Alternatively, you can add a second vmnic to the service console vSwitch. To suppress this message on hosts not configured in a VMware High Availability (HA) cluster, set the VMware HA advanced option das.ignoreRedundantNetWarning to true. If the warning appears for a host already configured in a cluster, set the option advanced option das.ignoreRedundantNetWarning to true and reconfigure VMware HA on that host. Note: If the warning continues to appear, disable and re-enable VMware High Availability in the cluster. To set das.ignoreRedundantNetWarning to true: From vSphere Client, right-click on the cluster and select Edit Settings. Select VMware HA and click Advanced Options. In the Options column, enter das.ignoreRedundantNetWarning. In the Value column, enter true. Note: Steps 3 and 4 create a new option. Click OK.
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host primary agent not in short name
knowledgeSummary: This event records that the primary agent specified is not a short name
knowledgeCause: Primary agent was not specified as a short name to host
knowledgeResolution: The name of the primary agent is usually stored as a short name. Check the network configurations of your hosts
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host reconnection failed
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failed attempt to re-establish a host connection
knowledgeCause: Cannot reconnect to host
knowledgeResolution: Try to reconnect the host manually. From the vSphere Client connected to a vCenter Server system, display the inventory and click the managed host to reconnect. Right-click the host and select Connect from the pop-up menu. When the managed host's connection status to vCenter Server is changed, the statuses of the VMs on that managed host are updated to reflect the change. If you are unable to reconnect to the host, check network settings, host status, etc - there may be other alerts generated that will indicate the root cause of the issue
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host short name inconsistent

name: Host short name IP resolve failed

name: License is not compliant

name: License file restricted

name: Locker misconfiguration

name: Insufficient user access permissions
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failed user logon due to insufficient access permission
knowledgeCause: Cannot login user: no permission. In vSphere, permission consists of a user or group and an assigned role for an inventory object, such as a VM or a host. Permissions grant users the right to perform the activities specified by the role on the object to which the role is assigned
knowledgeResolution: Check permissions for this user. Check for some automated process that is logging in with incorrect assigned privileges within vCenter Server. In worst-case scenario, check for denial-of-service attack or other attempted security hack

name: No license reservation
knowledgeSummary: These are events reported by License Manager.A NoLicenseEvent is reported if the required licenses could not be reserved. Each feature that is not fully licensed is reported

knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: No maintenance mode DRS recommendation for VM
knowledgeSummary: This event records that DRS did not recommend a migration for a powered on VM, even though its host is going into maintenance mode
knowledgeCause: Unable to automatically migrate VM.DRS may not be able to recommend a migration for a VM for reasons, including but not limited to:No other connected host is compatible with this VM.None of the other compatible hosts have sufficient resources to satisfy the reservation requirements of this VM.Moving to any other host would violate a DRS rule. For example, all other compatible hosts have some incompatible VMs running.DRS is disabled on this VM.This VM was still in the process of migrating into the host going into maintenance mode and was not considered by DRS.This VM was in the process of migrating to another host when the host tried to enter maintenance mode
knowledgeResolution: Check compatibility of other hosts in the cluster for this VMCheck available resources on other hosts in the clusterCheck availability of other hosts (e.g. Maintenance Mode, Standby Mode)Check configuration of DRS rules
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Not enough resources for failover
knowledgeSummary: This event records when the HA does not find sufficient resources to failover a VM
knowledgeCause: Not enough resources to failover VMIf you power on a VM and HA is enabled, vCenter Server first checks whether there are enough resources to continue supporting the specified number of host failovers if you power on the VM.If there are enough resources, the VM is powered on.If there are not enough resources and strict admission control is being used (the default), a message informs you that the VM cannot be powered on.If you are not using strict admission control, a message informs you that there are no longer enough resources to guarantee failover for all hosts. The VM is powered on but the cluster turns red.
knowledgeResolution: Check for available resources.Review HA cluster configuration.

name: DVS host configuration out of sync

name: Resource pool configuration conflict

knowledgeCause: Resource usage exceeds configuration for resource pool.VM's peak resource usage can exceed its reserved capacity by "borrowing" from extra floating cluster capacity or other "reserved" resources, if those resources are not currently being used. However this can indicate configuration or resource issues within vCenter Server
knowledgeResolution: Check your resource pool settings e.g. reservation and limit, and adjust if needed

name: vCenter Server license expired

name: VM clone operation failure

name: VM configuration file missing

knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM HA reset
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a VM is reset by HA VM Health Monitoring on hosts that do not support the create screenshot APIi or if the create screenshot API fails
knowledgeCause: The VM was reset due to a guest OS error.VM failure monitoring can monitor and reset a VM (note - only if VMware Tools is installed on that VM).When you enable VM failure monitoring, VMware HA monitors whether VMs are available. VMware HA uses the heartbeat information that VMware Tools captures to determine VM availability. On each VM, VMware Tools sends a heartbeat every second. VM failure monitoring checks for a heartbeat every 20 seconds. If heartbeats have not been received within a specified (user configurable) time interval, VM failure monitoring declares that VM as failed and resets the VM. VM failure monitoring can distinguish between a VM that was powered on but has stopped sending heartbeats and a VM that is powered off, suspended, or migrated
knowledgeResolution: VM HA monitoring uses the tools heartbeat as an indication of guest health state.Try to disable VM health monitoring on the VM (go the cluster settings -> VMware HA -> VM Monitoring -> configure VM appropriately in the VM Settings).Alternatively, decrease the monitoring sensitivity of VMs so HA will only reset them if the tools heartbeat is lost for a longer period

name: VM Screenshot HA reset
knowledgeSummary: This event records when a VM is reset by HA VM health monitoring on hosts that support the create screenshot API
knowledgeCause: VM reset due to a guest OS error. Screenshot is saved.VM failure monitoring can monitor and reset a VM (note - only if VMware Tools is installed on that VM)When you enable VM failure monitoring, VMware HA monitors whether VMs are available. VMware HA uses the heartbeat information that VMware Tools captures to determine VM availability. On each VM, VMware Tools sends a heartbeat every second. VM Failure Monitoring checks for a heartbeat every 20 seconds. If heartbeats have not been received within a specified (user configurable) time interval, VM failure monitoring declares that VM as failed and resets the VM.VM failure monitoring can distinguish between a VM that was powered on but has stopped sending heartbeats and a VM that is powered off, suspended, or migrated
knowledgeResolution: VM HA monitoring uses the tools heartbeat as an indication of guest health state.Try to disable VM health monitoring on the VM (go the cluster settings -> VMware HA -> VM Monitoring -> configure VM appropriately in the VM Settings).Alternatively, decrease the monitoring sensitivity of VMs so HA will only reset them if the tools heartbeat is lost for a longer period

name: VM HA reset failure
knowledgeSummary: This event records when HA VM health monitoring fails to reset a VM after failure
knowledgeCause: Cannot reset VM due to a guest OS error.When you enable VM failure monitoring, VMware HA monitors whether VMs are available. VMware HA uses the heartbeat information that VMware Tools captures to determine VM availability. On each VM, VMware Tools sends a heartbeat every second. VM failure monitoring checks for a heartbeat every 20 seconds. If heartbeats have not been received within a specified (user configurable) time interval, VM failure monitoring declares that VM as failed and resets the VM.VM failure monitoring can distinguish between a VM that was powered on but has stopped sending heartbeats and a VM that is powered off, suspended, or migrated
knowledgeResolution: Try resetting VM manually

name: HA agent update failed
knowledgeSummary: The event records an error occurred when updating the HA agents with the current state of the VM
knowledgeCause: Unable to update HA agents given the state of a VM.VM failure monitoring can monitor and reset a VM (note - only if VMware Tools is installed on that VM).When you enable VM failure monitoring, VMware HA monitors whether VMs are available. VMware HA uses the heartbeat information that VMware Tools captures to determine VM availability. On each VM, VMware Tools sends a heartbeat every second. VM Failure Monitoring checks for a heartbeat every 20 seconds. If heartbeats have not been received within a specified (user configurable) time interval, VM Failure Monitoring declares that the VM has failed and resets the VM.VM failure monitoring can distinguish between a VM that was powered on but has stopped sending heartbeats and a VM that is powered off, suspended, or migrated
knowledgeResolution: If this occurs during a powerOn operation, the VM will not fail over in the event of a host failure. If it occurs during a powerOff, the VM will be automatically powered on if the host it was last running on crashes. Check the VM State. Review HA event log and any additional alerts for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Template deployment failure
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failure to deploy a VM from a template
knowledgeCause: Cannot deploy a template
knowledgeResolution: Validate that each troubleshooting step below is true for your environment. Each step will provide instructions or a link to a document, in order to eliminate possible causes and take corrective action as necessary. The steps are ordered in the most appropriate sequence to isolate the issue and identify the proper resolution. Name resolution problems in the host environment can cause problems with file copies. Verify that Name Resolution is valid on the host. Refer to this VMware KB article for more information.The correct version of sysprep is critical in deployments. Verify that the correct version of Microsoft sysprep has been installed to properly do the customization. Refer to this VMware KB article for more information.In some cases, other VMware products can interfere with the normal operation of vCenter Server. Verify that no other VMware products have been installed on the vCenter Server. Refer to this VMware KB article for more information.Verify that the guest operating system is defined correctly. Incorrect definitions can cause deployment failures. Refer to this VMware KB article for more information.Determine if the problem is being caused due to conditions related to slow cloning or deployment of templates. Refer to this VMware KB article for more information.
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Virtual disk creation failed
knowledgeSummary: This event records a failure to create a virtual disk in a VM
knowledgeCause: Cannot create virtual diskWhen you add a hard disk to a VM, you can create a new virtual disk, add an existing virtual disk, or add a mapped SAN LUN
knowledgeResolution: Check user permissions on file system where virtual disk should be created Check VMFS block size format Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Secondary VM failed to start
knowledgeSummary: The Secondary VM cannot be powered on as there are no compatible hosts that can accommodate it
knowledgeCause: When enabling VMware Fault Tolerance and the primary is powered on, VMware Fault Tolerance automatically attempts to start the fault tolerant secondary. Starting the secondary fails after a brief delay and produces this error.If HV (Hardware Virtualization) is enabled for the host that is hosting a primary copy of a fault tolerant VM, but not on any other hosts in the cluster, the primary can be successfully powered on, but this error is produced. If the other hosts do not have HV enabled, but the primary does, the primary VM is still powered on but produces the error message
knowledgeResolution: In this case the primary remains powered on in live mode, but fault tolerance is not established. VMware FT requires that Hardware Virtualization (HV) be turned on in the BIOS

name: Secondary VM config update failed
knowledgeSummary: This event is recorded after a failover of the new primary VM failed to update the config of the secondary VM
knowledgeCause: vCenter Server cannot update the Secondary VM configuration
knowledgeResolution: Check the status, availability and Event log on the Secondary VM

name: Fault Tolerance VM terminated
knowledgeSummary: This event records a secondary or primary VM is terminated
knowledgeCause: The reason could be: divergence, lost connection to the secondary, partial hardware failure of the secondary, or by user intervention
knowledgeResolution: Check the reasons specified in Cause section

name: VM guest reboot
knowledgeSummary: This is a VM guest reboot request event
knowledgeCause: Guest OS is rebooted for the VM.A host can signal the guest service to shut down or restart a VM. After the guest service receives a request to shut down or restart, it sends an acknowledgment back to the host
knowledgeResolution: If this is a desired admin action then no resolution is required. This could be done by any user who has sufficient permissions. Note that only if initiated via vSphere Client UI (Reboot Guest) will this be captured. Usage of Shutdown or Reboot from within the guest OS (console, RDP session etc) will not generate this event
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM guest shutdown
knowledgeSummary: This is a VM guest shutdown request event
knowledgeCause: Guest OS is shut down on a VM.A host can signal the guest service to shut down or restart a VM. After the guest service receives a request to shut down or restart, it sends an acknowledgment back to the host
knowledgeResolution: If this is a desired admin action, then no resolution is required. This could be done by any user who has sufficient permissions. Note that only if initiated via vSphere UI (Reboot Guest) will this be captured. Usage of Shutdown or Reboot from within the guest OS (console, RDP session etc) will not generate this event
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM instance UUID conflict
knowledgeSummary: This event records a conflict of VM instance UUIDs
knowledgeCause: The instance UUID of VM conflicts with the instance UUID assigned to another VM.A number used to uniquely identify some object or entity. The UUID is either assigned by VMware vSphere (in the case of VMs) or is hardware-assigned (in the case of SCSI LUNs). vCenter Server attempts to ensure that the UUIDs of all VMs being managed are unique, changing the UUIDs of conflicting VMs if necessary
knowledgeResolution: Refer to this VMware KB article and VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM MAC address conflict
knowledgeSummary: This event records a MAC address conflict for a VM
knowledgeCause: The MAC address of a VM conflicts with the MAC assigned to another VM.Note that this event has been observed during failover of FT (Fault-Tolerant) VMs
knowledgeResolution: If this occurs during Fault Tolerant VM failover, then no action may be required. In other cases, check configuration and status of VM and vCenter Server.Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM generic error
knowledgeSummary: This is a generic event for error messages from a VM that do not fit into any other specific vCenter Server event
knowledgeCause: Error message on a VM. This event records when an error message (consisting of a collection of "observations") is thrown by the VM
knowledgeResolution: Check alert description and other associated alerts for this VM for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM generic warning
knowledgeSummary: This is a generic event for warning messages from a VM that did not fit into any other specific vCenter Server event
knowledgeCause: Warning message on a VM. This event is recorded when a warning message (consisting of a collection of "observations") is thrown by the VM
knowledgeResolution: Check the alert description and other associated alerts for this VM for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: No compatible host for Secondary VM
knowledgeSummary: This event records that no compatible host was found to place a secondary VM. A default alarm will be triggered upon this event, which by default, would trigger a SNMP trap
knowledgeCause: No compatible host for the Secondary VM.This can occur for a variety of reasons including that there are no other hosts in the cluster, there are no other hosts with HV enabled, datastores are inaccessible, insufficient capacity, or hosts are in maintenance mode
knowledgeResolution: If there are insufficient hosts, add more hosts to the cluster. If there are hosts in the cluster, ensure they support HV and that HV is enabled. The process for enabling HV varies among BIOSes. See the documentation for your hosts' BIOSes for details on how to enable HV. Check that hosts have sufficient capacity and that they are not in maintenance mode

name: No network access for VM migration
knowledgeSummary: This event records a migration failure when the destination host is not on the same network as the source host
knowledgeCause: Not all networks for the VM are accessible by the host
knowledgeResolution: Refer to this VMware KB article and VMware Virtual Networking Concepts

name: No host for a virtual machine available
knowledgeSummary: This event records a VM for which no host is responsible

knowledgeResolution: Check that host and datastore exists and connection is properly configured. Check VM registration in vCenter Server
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: vMotion license expired
knowledgeSummary: This event records an expired vMotion license
knowledgeCause: A vMotion license for a host has expired
knowledgeResolution: Update your license key to allow vMotion operations
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: FT VM Failover
knowledgeSummary: This event records a fault tolerance failover

name: VM resetting
knowledgeSummary: This event records a VM resetting
knowledgeCause: A VM is being reset

name: VM restart on alternate host

name: Secondary VM start timeout
knowledgeSummary: This event records timeout when starting a secondary VM
knowledgeCause: Starting the secondary VM timed out
knowledgeResolution: You might be experiencing network latency that is causing the timeout.Refer to this VMware KB article

name: VM WWN conflict
knowledgeSummary: This event records a conflict of VM WWNs (World Wide Name)
knowledgeCause: The WWN of VM conflicts with the currently registered WWN

name: Guest disk space
knowledgeSummary: Guest OS volume is low on available guest disk space
knowledgeCause: Guest OS free disk space value has dropped below the configured threshold
knowledgeResolution: Increase disk space and extend guest OS volume, or free up some disk space by removing unused programs or files. For Windows guests, you can use Disk Cleanup Wizard

name: Latest snapshot size
knowledgeSummary: The VM has been running on a snapshot for extended period of time, which exceeded the configured threshold for the snapshot file size.A snapshot preserves the state and data of a VM at a specific point in time, and is typically used for backup of running VMs or maintenance activities (such as patching)
knowledgeCause: A VM provides several operations for creating and managing snapshots and snapshot chains. These operations let you create snapshots, revert to any snapshot in the chain, and remove snapshots. You can create extensive snapshot trees that you can use to save VM state at any point in time, and restore it later, if necessary. It is common that users forget to remove snapshot when it is no longer needed.Additional, 3rd party software (such as backup software) can automatically create snapshots for the purpose of hot backup. Sometimes, such software may be unable to automatically remove the snapshot due to intermittent network or vCenter Server communication issue, leaving the snapshot behind.If a VM is running off of a snapshot, it is storing all disk changes to a child disk. The more changes are made to the VM disks, the larger the snapshot file grows. Snapshot file size can never exceed the size of original VMDK file though
knowledgeResolution: Please check with Snapshot Manager for any open snapshots running and delete them if needed. Additionally, review VMware's KB article about managing snapshots

name: Datastore free space
knowledgeSummary: Datastore is low on available free space
knowledgeCause: Datastore free space value dropped below the configured threshold
knowledgeResolution: Increase datastore capacity (for example, extend the corresponding LUN), or alternatively free up some space by migrating VMs to another datastore, deleting unused VMs, and removing snapshots
knowledgeExternal: For further instructions about extending a VMFS Volume to use new LUN space after expansion see VMware's KB article

name: Datastore over-allocation
knowledgeSummary: Datastore is over-allocated
knowledgeCause: Disk space allocated by thin-provisioned VMs exceeds the configured threshold
knowledgeResolution: Increase datastore disk capacity size or migrate some VMs to another datastore

name: Host connectivity failure
knowledgeSummary: This event indicates that one or more portgroups in the host have lost connectivity to the network, resulting in unavailability of all physical connections to the network from this switch
knowledgeCause: Failure of the network cable, disconnect of cable, failure of the physical network card, failure of network switch/port/etc
knowledgeResolution: To determine the actual failure or to eliminate possible issues:Check the status of the vmnic from either the VMware vSphere Client or the ESX host service console:To check the status from the vSphere Client:Select the host and click the Configuration tabClick NetworkingThe vmnics currently assigned to virtual switches are displayed in the diagrams. If a vmnic displays a red X, that link is currently downTo check the status from the ESX host service console, run the following command [root@server root]# esxcfg-nics -lThe output appears similar to the following: Name PCI Driver Link Speed Duplex Description vmnic0 04:04.00 tg3 Up 1000Mbps Full Broadcom BCM5780 Gigabit Ethernet vmnic1 04:04.01 tg3 Up 1000Mbps Full Broadcom BCM5780 Gigabit EthernetThe Link column specifies the status of the link between the network adapter and the physical switch. The status can be either Up or Down. If there are several network adapters, with some being up and some down, you might have to verify that they are connected to the intended physical switch ports. This can be done by bringing down each of the ESX host's ports on the physical switch and running the esxcfg-nics -l command to observe which vmnic is affected.Check that the vmnic referred to in the event message is still connected to the switch and configured properly:Make sure that the network cable is still connected to the switch and to the host.Check that the switch connected to the system is still functioning properly and has not been misconfigured. Refer to the switch documentation for details.Check for activity between the physical switch and the vmnic. This might be indicated either by a network trace or activity LEDs.Check for network port settings on the physical switch.Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host redundancy failure
knowledgeSummary: The event indicates that one or more portgroups in the host has lost a redundant uplink to the physical network. Portgroups are still connected. However this may be the last redundant uplink. Check the event description and context to confirm the status
knowledgeCause: Failure of the network cable, disconnect of cable, failure of the physical network card, failure of network switch/port/etc
knowledgeResolution: To determine the actual failure or to eliminate possible issues:Check the status of the vmnic from either the VMware vSphere Client or the ESX host service console:To check the status from the vSphere Client:Select the host and click the Configuration tabClick NetworkingThe vmnics currently assigned to virtual switches are displayed in the diagrams. If a vmnic displays a red X, that link is currently downTo check the status from the ESX host service console, run the following command [root@server root]# esxcfg-nics -lThe output appears similar to the following: Name PCI Driver Link Speed Duplex Description vmnic0 04:04.00 tg3 Up 1000Mbps Full Broadcom BCM5780 Gigabit Ethernet vmnic1 04:04.01 tg3 Up 1000Mbps Full Broadcom BCM5780 Gigabit EthernetThe Link column specifies the status of the link between the network adapter and the physical switch. The status can be either Up or Down. If there are several network adapters, with some being up and some down, you might have to verify that they are connected to the intended physical switch ports. This can be done by bringing down each of the ESX host's ports on the physical switch and running the esxcfg-nics -l command to observe which vmnic is affected.Check that the vmnic referred to in the event message is still connected to the switch and configured properly:Make sure that the network cable is still connected to the switch and to the host.Check that the switch connected to the system is still functioning properly and has not been misconfigured. Refer to the switch documentation for details.Check for activity between the physical switch and the vmnic. This might be indicated either by a network trace or activity LEDs.Check for network port settings on the physical switch.Refer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Storage connection failure
knowledgeSummary: The event indicates a loss in connectivity to the specified storage device. The path indicated is the last path that went down
knowledgeCause: Failure of the storage connection cable, disconnect of cable, failure of the physical storage adapter, failure of disk controller/port/etc
knowledgeResolution: The storage device reading (vmhba35:C1:T0:L7) mentioned in the example earlier contains several potential failure points:vmhba35 - HBA (Host Bus Adapter)C1 - ChannelT0 - Target (Storage processor port)L7 - LUN (Logical Unit Number or Disk Unit)To determine the actual failure or to eliminate possible issues:Identify the available storage paths to the reported storage device by running the esxcfg-mpath -l command. For more information, see Obtaining LUN pathing information for ESX Server 3 (1003973)Check that a rescan does not restore visibility to the targets. Follow the steps provided in Performing a rescan of the storage (1003988) to rescan the storage device by using the command-line interface and VMware vSphere Client.Determine whether the connectivity issue is with the iSCSI storage or the fiber storage. Perform one of the following depending on what your connectivity issue is:To troubleshoot the connectivity to the iSCSI storage using the software initiatorCheck whether a ping to the storage array fails from the host. For more information, see Testing VMkernel network connectivity with the vmkping command (1003728).Check that the initiator is registered on the array. Contact your storage vendor for instructions on this procedure.Check that the following physical hardware is correctly functioning:Ethernet switchEthernet cables between the switch and the hostEthernet cables between the switch and the storage arrayTo troubleshoot the connectivity to the fiber attached storage, check the following:The fiber switch zoning configuration permits the host to see the storage array. Consult your switch vendor if you require assistance.The fiber switch propagates RSCN messages to the hosts. For more information on configuring the fiber switch. Configuring fibre switch so that ESX Server doesn't require a reboot after a zone set change (1002301).Check the physical hardware for the following:The storage processors on the array.The fiber switch and the Gigabit Interface Converter (GBIC) units in the switch.The fiber cables between the fiber switch and the array.The array itself.Important: A rescan is required after any change is made to see if the targets are detectedRefer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Storage connection redundancy failure
knowledgeSummary: A host has lost a path to access the specified storage and the path to storage is either degraded, or no longer redundant. Check the event description and context to confirm the status
knowledgeCause: Failure of the storage connection cable, disconnect of cable, failure of the physical storage adapter, failure of disk controller/port/etc
knowledgeResolution: If vmhba35:C1:T0:L7 is one of two active paths to access the storage device and the path goes down, an error of the following type is logged: "Path redundancy to storage device naa.60a9800043346534645a433967325334 lost. Path vmhba35:C1:T0:L7 is down"This example of a storage device reading contains several potential failure points:vmhba35 - HBA (Host Bus Adapter)C1 - ControllerT0 - Target (Storage Processor)L7 - LUN NumberTo determine the cause of the failure:Identify the available storage paths to the reported storage device by running the esxcfg-mpath -l command. For more information, see Obtaining LUN pathing information for ESX Server 3 (1003973)Check that a rescan does not restore visibility to the targets. Follow the steps provided in Performing a rescan of the storage (1003988) to rescan the storage device by using the command-line interface and VMware vSphere Client.Determine whether the connectivity issue is with the iSCSI storage or the fiber storage. Perform one of the following depending on what your connectivity issue is:To troubleshoot the connectivity to the iSCSI storage using the software initiatorCheck whether a ping to the storage array fails from the host. For more information, see Testing VMkernel network connectivity with the vmkping command (1003728).Check that the initiator is registered on the array. Contact your storage vendor for instructions on this procedure.Check that the following physical hardware is correctly functioning:Ethernet switchEthernet cables between the switch and the hostEthernet cables between the switch and the storage arrayTo troubleshoot the connectivity to the fiber attached storage, check the following:The fiber switch zoning configuration permits the host to see the storage array. Consult your switch vendor if you require assistance.The fiber switch propagates RSCN messages to the hosts. For more information on configuring the fiber switch. Configuring fibre switch so that ESX Server doesn't require a reboot after a zone set change (1002301).Check the physical hardware for the following:The storage processors on the array.The fiber switch and the Gigabit Interface Converter (GBIC) units in the switch.The fiber cables between the fiber switch and the array.The array itself.Important: A rescan is required after any change is made to see if the targets are detectedRefer to this VMware KB article for more information
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Host hardware status
knowledgeSummary: One of the hosts' hardware sensors has changed its status
knowledgeCause: Monitoring hardware sensor information allows tracking physical hardware status. Sensor's status is automatically refreshed by vCenter Server's request
knowledgeResolution: For more information on the hardware sensor please navigate to Hardware tab in Veeam ONE Client. Also check the hardware on the host triggered the alarm
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation and to corresponding KB article for more information

name: Heartbeat is missing for VM
knowledgeSummary: The heartbeat is the communication to the VMware tools heartbeat running inside the VM.Heartbeat can only be monitored when the VMware tools are installed in a VM. The heartbeat is what vCenter Server uses to determine the general health and availability of a running VM
knowledgeCause: A heartbeat alarm means that the vCenter Server is getting a weak heartbeat from the VM or the heartbeat has disappeared altogether.This can be caused by a problem with the VMware Tools service status itself, or can indicate that although the VM is running, the guestOS has failed (for example, Blue Screen)
knowledgeResolution: Check the status of the guestOS in the VM, and the VMware Tools service status
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: VM power status
knowledgeSummary: The power state of a VM indicates whether the VM is active and functional
knowledgeCause: There are several access points for making changes to power states:Selecting the virtual machine and the power option from the Inventory > Virtual Machine menuSelecting Power on from the Commands areaSelecting the power option from the right-click menuScheduling a power state change using the Scheduled Tasks button in the navigation bar
knowledgeResolution: When a power operation is performed on a VM, the VM power state changes and all other commands are locked out until the first command is completed.To manage VM's power state select one of the power options below:Power on Powers on the VM and boots the guest operating system if the guest operating system is installedPower off Powers off the VM. The VM does not attempt to shut down the guest operating system gracefully.Suspend Pauses the VM activity. All VM operations are frozen until you issue a resume command.
knowledgeExternal: Refer to VMware's online documentation for more information

name: Latest snapshot age
knowledgeSummary: The age of the (latest) snapshot for this Virtual Machine has exceeded the configured threshold
knowledgeCause: This alarm alerts on virtual machines which have a snapshot that is older than 'x' hours
knowledgeResolution: Remove the snapshot from this VM (using the vCenter Client). If it is desired to retain this snapshot, this alarm can be disabled. Alternatively, the age threshold for this alarm can be changed

name: Virtual Server connection failure
knowledgeSummary: Connection to Virtual Server has failed
knowledgeCause: Virtual Server might not be available
knowledgeResolution: Verify that Virtual Server is availableMake sure firewall on Virtual Server allows incoming connectionsCheck account permissions used to connect to Virtual Server specifiedUse native tools to test connection to specified Virtual Server

name: Topology collection failure
knowledgeSummary: Veeam ONE Monitoring Server failed to collect infrastructure topology
knowledgeCause: Virtual Server might not be accessible
knowledgeResolution: Make sure Virtual Server is accessible
knowledgeExternal: See a corresponding event description for more information

name: Performance data collection failure
knowledgeSummary: Veeam ONE Monitoring Server failed to collect performance data from the objects specified
knowledgeCause: There might be multiple reasons for this:Virtual Server might not be accessibleVirtual Server is under heavy load
knowledgeResolution: Verify that Virtual Server is availableMake sure firewall on Virtual Server allows incoming connectionsCheck account permissions used to connect to Virtual Server specifiedUse native tools to test connection to specified server
knowledgeExternal: See a corresponding event description for more information

name: Events data collection failure
knowledgeSummary: Veeam ONE Monitoring Server failed to collect events data from the objects specified
knowledgeCause: There might be multiple reasons for this:Virtual Server might not be accessibleVirtual Server is under heavy load
knowledgeResolution: Verify that Virtual Server is availableMake sure firewall on Virtual Server allows incoming connectionsCheck account permissions used to connect to Virtual Server specifiedUse native tools to test connection to specified server
knowledgeExternal: See a corresponding event description for more information

name: Datastore is inaccessible
knowledgeSummary: The event indicates a loss in connectivity to the specified storage device. The path indicated is the last path that went down
knowledgeCause: Failure of the storage connection cable, disconnect of cable, failure of the physical storage adapter, failure of disk controller/port/etc
knowledgeResolution: The storage device reading (vmhba35:C1:T0:L7) mentioned in the example earlier contains several potential failure points:vmhba35 - HBA (Host Bus Adapter)C1 - ChannelT0 - Target (Storage processor port)L7 - LUN (Logical Unit Number or Disk Unit)To determine the actual failure or to eliminate possible issues:Identify the available storage paths to the reported storage device by running the esxcfg-mpath -l command. For more information, see Obtaining LUN pathing information for ESX Server 3 (1003973)Check that a rescan does not restore visibility to the targets. Follow the steps provided in Performing a rescan of the storage (1003988) to rescan the storage device by using the command-line interface and VMware vSphere Client.Determine whether the connectivity issue is with the iSCSI storage or the fiber storage.
